content.text:
I found the root cause. Here it is in plain English.

## Why the page looks unchanged

AG Grid (the big table) can be styled two different ways:
- the **old way** — CSS targeting `.ag-theme-alpine`
- the **new way** — the "Theming API" (a `themeQuartz.withParams({...})` object in code)

**This app is set to use the new way.** Every grid defaults to the `quartz` theme through a shared theme service (`grid.theme.service.ts` → defaults to `quartz`). And the spec you were handed says this *in bold*:

> **SPEC_V2 §2:** "AG Grid v35 Theming API is active via the shared theme service… CSS variables (`--ag-*`) and `.ag-theme-alpine` overrides **do nothing here**."

But the previous session wrote the **entire color/font reskin the old way** — as `::ng-deep .ag-theme-alpine { --ag-background-color: … }` in `rfq.component.scss`. It did that because a note in the middle of your prompt told it to ("the grid is on legacy Alpine, use CSS variables instead"). **That note was wrong — it contradicts the spec.** So:

- ✅ The **status dot** and **step dot** show up — those are little code components (cell renderers), independent of theming.
- ❌ The **colors, backgrounds, fonts, header styling** — the 99.5% — were written against a mechanism that "does nothing" in this app, so they never reach the screen.

The code exists and is committed; it's just plugged into the wrong socket. That's why it builds, the dot appears, but the reskin is invisible.

## The fix (what the spec actually wants)

1. Put all the colors/typography into the **Theming API** — build `themePink = themeQuartz.withParams({...})` in `grid-themes.config.ts` using the exact §4 table.
2. **Actually bind that theme to the RFQ grid** — add `[theme]` to the `<ag-grid-angular>` (and the detail sub-grid), instead of `class="ag-theme-alpine"`.
3. Keep the structural `::ng-deep` rules (header heights, seam, scenario tints — the spec says those are fine), and **delete the inert `::ng-deep .ag-theme-alpine { --ag-* }` block**.
4. Re-check against `RFQ Prototype.html`.

This is a real chunk of work, but it's well-defined — and it's the thing that will make the page finally look like the prototype.

Before I start changing code, two things genuinely affect how I proceed — I don't want to guess on either:

**Query:** apply Theming API theme to ag-grid-angular [theme] input, themeQuartz withParams, migrate from legacy ag-theme-alpine CSS, error 239 legacy CSS and Theming API together
**Version:** 35.1.0
**Framework:** angular
**Total Results:** 10

---

AG Grid provides three main approaches to styling:


* **Theming API:** Fully-typed API for customising default themes.
* **Custom CSS:** Fine-grained control over specific grid elements.
* **Cell/Row Styles:** Data-driven formatting for individual cells and rows.


Each of these approaches can be used to customise our built-in themes: [Quartz](https://www.ag-grid.com/example), [Alpine](https://www.ag-grid.com/example?theme=alpine), [Balham](https://www.ag-grid.com/example?theme=balham), and [Material](https://www.ag-grid.com/example?theme=material).


Our themes are simply JavaScript objects that define colours, spacing, fonts, and other design tokens. By default, the Quartz theme is applied; to use a custom theme, create a reference to the desired theme and pass it to the grid options:


```
import { Component } from '@angular/core';
import { AgGridAngular } from 'ag-grid-angular';
import { themeQuartz } from 'ag-grid-community';

@Component({
    selector: 'app-root',
    template: `
        <ag-grid-angular
            [theme]="theme"
            <!-- other options -->
        />
    `,
})
export class AppComponent {
    theme = themeQuartz;
}
```


The example below demonstrates an AG Grid data grid with an unmodified Quartz theme. Open the example in CodeSandbox or Plunkr to follow this tutorial:


```app.component.ts
import { Component } from "@angular/core";

import { AgGridAngular } from "ag-grid-angular";
import type { ColDef, ValueFormatterParams } from "ag-grid-community";
import {
  ClientSideRowModelModule,
  ModuleRegistry,
  NumberFilterModule,
  RowSelectionModule
...

content.text:
I now have the authoritative AG Grid v35 guidance, and it confirms the diagnosis precisely:

- **Default theme is `themeQuartz`** (Theming API). Since the app sets no `theme` option *anywhere* and never calls `provideGlobalGridOptions({theme:'legacy'})`, **every grid renders as Quartz** — the `ag-theme-alpine` class and imported legacy CSS are vestigial, and `--ag-*` overrides on `.ag-theme-alpine` do nothing. Exactly what SPEC_V2 §2 says.
- **Fix = bind `[theme]="themeQuartz.withParams({…})"`** on the `<ag-grid-angular>`, scoped to RFQ only (won't touch other grids).

content.text:
I found why it's stuck — and it's a networking constraint, not a problem with the fix.

The browser console shows:

> **`BrowserAuthError: crypto_nonexistent`** (MSAL / Microsoft login)

Microsoft login (MSAL) requires a **secure context**, which the browser only grants to `https://…` or to **`http://localhost`**. The Chrome I'm connected to (**office-XPS**) is a *different computer* than the one running the dev server, so it can only reach the server by its network address `http://10.0.5.55:4200` — and at a plain `http://<ip>` address the browser disables the crypto API, so Microsoft login can't initialize and the app hangs on the splash screen. A browser opening `http://localhost:4200` **on the same PC as the server** would not hit this.

So the fix itself is fine; the screenshot path is blocked by *where* the browser is. I need one piece of info to pick the right route:

content.text:
The login page is up in your on-PC Chrome (`localhost:4200/login`). I can't enter credentials for you, so please **sign in now** in that window — either with email/password or **"Sign in with Microsoft."**

Once you're signed in, just reply "done" and I'll navigate to the RFQ page and capture the before/after for you.

While you do that, here's where things stand: the full Theming-API fix is implemented and compiling cleanly, and the new unit test passes. Specifically I:

- Built the real **`themePink = themeQuartz.withParams({…})`** with all the SPEC §4 tokens (brand pink accent, white/zebra rows, pink hover/selection, slate text, header styling, borders, Hanken Grotesk font) in [grid-themes.config.ts](src/app/helpers/ag-grid/grid-themes.config.ts).
- **Bound it to the grid** with `[theme]="rfqTheme"` in [rfq.component.html](src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.html) and the detail sub-grid in [detail-cell-renderer.component.html](src/app/pages/rfq/detail-cell-renderer/detail-cell-renderer.component.html) — this is the part that was missing.
- **Removed** the inert `::ng-deep .ag-theme-alpine { --ag-* }` block (it did nothing), kept all the structural `::ng-deep` rules.
- Added the **Hanken Grotesk + IBM Plex Mono** fonts in [index.html](src/index.html).
- Added a unit test ([grid-themes.config.spec.ts](src/app/helpers/ag-grid/grid-themes.config.spec.ts)) — passes, and all existing RFQ specs still pass.

Reply **done** once you're logged in and I'll grab the screenshot.

content: it keeps popping up on a different computer

"Cannot reach the Designflow server while trying to complete Azure. Check your connection, VPN/proxy, or regional network access, then try again. Technical details: browser reported HTTP status 0; URL: https://api.alsand2.designflow.app/api/core/auth/azure."
do you need to add anything to azure SSO?

message.content:
it keeps popping up on a different computer
"Cannot reach the Designflow server while trying to complete Azure. Check your connection, VPN/proxy, or regional network access, then try again. Technical details: browser reported HTTP status 0; URL: https://api.alsand2.designflow.app/api/core/auth/azure."
do you need to add anything to azure SSO?

content.text:
Good diagnostic detail — that error answers your question. **It's not Azure SSO that's blocking you.** Here's what `HTTP status 0` on `https://api.alsand2.designflow.app/api/core/auth/azure` means:

- The **Microsoft sign-in part worked** — you got past Microsoft. The failure is the app's *next* step: calling the **backend** to exchange your Microsoft token for a Designflow session.
- `HTTP status 0` is a **CORS/network block**, not a login rejection. The hosted backend (`api.alsand2.designflow.app`) doesn't allow requests coming from the `http://localhost:4200` origin. When you use the real site (`alsand2.designflow.app`) that origin is allowed; `localhost` is not.

So the thing to "add" is **not** in Azure — it's either (a) adding `http://localhost:4200` to the **backend's** CORS allowlist (a change in `designflow-backend`/infra, not this repo), or (b) a **local dev proxy** that makes the browser talk only to `localhost:4200` and forwards API calls to the hosted backend, which sidesteps CORS entirely. I can do (b) myself right now — no Azure or backend changes needed.

(For completeness: the *Azure* redirect URI `http://localhost:4200` does need to exist in the App Registration for the Microsoft step — but since you got past Microsoft to the backend call, that part is evidently already fine.)

Given you're also fighting the login popup landing on the wrong computer, here's the decision:

questions.question: Local login is blocked by backend CORS (not Azure). How do you want to verify the reskin?

options.label: Set up dev proxy, retry local
options.description: I add an Angular dev proxy (forwards /api to the hosted backend, no CORS) so you can log in at localhost:4200 on this PC and I screenshot. Keeps it local, no Azure/backend changes.

options.label: Push & view on my real site
options.description: I commit/push to albert-2sandbox; your deploy pipeline puts it on alsand2.designflow.app where you're already logged in (no CORS, no popup hassle). You view it there.

options.label: Add localhost to backend CORS
options.description: You (or I, in the designflow-backend repo) add http://localhost:4200 to the backend CORS allowlist, then retry local login. Requires backend change + redeploy.

cd "D:/repos/dflow alsand2" && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(rfq): render the visual reskin via the v35 Theming API

The RFQ grid defaults to the Quartz Theming API (no theme:'legacy' is set
anywhere), so the SPEC_V2 §4 colour/typography reskin that had been written as
::ng-deep .ag-theme-alpine { --ag-* } overrides did nothing — only the cell
renderers (status pill / step dot) showed. Per SPEC_V2 §2/§4 and the AG Grid
v35 docs, move all colour/typography into themeQuartz.withParams (themePink) and
bind it to the grid with [theme].

- grid-themes.config.ts: full themePink withParams() per §4 (accent, row/zebra/
  hover/selection, slate text, header, borders, Hanken Grotesk, spacing); export
  themePink + themePinkDetail.
- rfq.component: bind [theme]="rfqTheme"; drop the inert ag-theme-alpine class.
- detail-cell-renderer: bind [theme]="detailTheme" on the factory-quote sub-grid.
- rfq.component.scss: remove the inert ::ng-deep .ag-theme-alpine { --ag-* }
  block; keep all structural ::ng-deep rules (heights, seam, tints).
- index.html: load Hanken Grotesk + IBM Plex Mono (§3).
- grid-themes.config.spec.ts: lock in the theme wiring.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -8

content.text:
No GitHub Actions in the repo, and `gh run list` is empty — so your deploy isn't GitHub-based. The repo has a `cloudbuild.yaml` + `Dockerfile` + `nginx.conf`, so the deploy almost certainly runs through **Google Cloud Build** (triggered on push to `albert-2sandbox`), which I can't watch from here. It typically takes a few minutes.

## Where things stand

✅ **Pushed to `albert-2sandbox`** ([5c6f9e72](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/commit/5c6f9e72)). The real fix is in: the reskin now goes through AG Grid's Theming API and is actually bound to the grid, which is the piece that was missing.

**To see it:** once your pipeline finishes deploying, open the **RFQ page on `alsand2.designflow.app`** (you're already logged in there — no CORS, no popup hassle) and **hard-refresh** with `Ctrl+Shift+R` to bypass the cache.

**What should now be visibly different** (not just the status dot):
- White rows with a subtle zebra stripe, **pink hover and selected-row** highlight
- Restyled headers — lighter background, uppercase, **Hanken Grotesk** font, brand-pink accents
- The blue/violet **scenario column tints** and group bands
- The **frozen identity pane** (Price Code · Pic · Description) with its seam shadow
- **Status pills** and **step dots**, the **density toggle** (Compact/Standard/Comfortable), and tighter toolbar buttons

## A couple of notes
- I committed only the code fix (8 files) — the `design_handoff_rfq_polish/` reference folder is left untracked, not pushed.

The grid simply isn't using themePink; it's rendering default Quartz (IBM Plex Sans is AG Grid's own default). The app stores the grid API on `window.gridApi`, so let me ask the running grid directly what theme it has bound.

content.text: Deploy is fully fresh (my index.html font links and `main-LJ6DYXCA.js` are live), so this is **not** a cache issue — my code is running, but something is **overriding** the `[theme]` input back to default Quartz at runtime. The prime suspect is a shared service called in `onGridReady` (`gridUISetupService.bootstrap`) or the theme service. Let me find any code that calls `setGridOption('theme', …)`.

content.text: **Found the root cause.** `src/@vex/services/grid-ui.service.ts` (lines 27, 32) calls `gridApi.setGridOption('theme', currentTheme.theme)` — and `bootstrap()` runs in RFQ's `onGridReady` *after* my `[theme]` binding. The shared service reads the theme service (which defaults to `quartz`) and **overwrites my themePink with bare Quartz on every grid**. Let me read the service to apply a clean, RFQ-scoped fix.

That's the bug. `bootstrapTheme()` sets the grid theme to the global default (`quartz`) and re-applies it on every theme-change event — clobbering my `[theme]="themePink"` input on every grid. The RFQ grid needs to **re-assert themePink after `bootstrap()`**. This is a one-spot, RFQ-only fix.

content.content: The file D:/repos/dflow alsand2/src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +    // Re-assert the RFQ brand theme (SPEC_V2 §4). gridUISetupService.bootstrap()
+    // above applies the app-wide default grid theme (quartz) via
+    // The RFQ grid is intentionally themed pink regardless of the global theme
+    // switcher, so set it back here after bootstrap has run.
// Apply the persisted row density once the grid API is available (SPEC_V2 §5i).

reskin never showed. Re-assert this.rfqTheme (themePink) after bootstrap runs so
the brand theme wins. Verified live: pink accent, pink selected row, restyled
headers, status pills, and the frozen-column seam now render.

content.text:
Done — pushed the permanent fix ([4fb92f65](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/commit/4fb92f65)).

## What was actually wrong (and now fixed)

The reskin code was correct the whole time — the problem was a **timing/override bug** I could only find by inspecting the live grid:

- The app has a shared service, `GridUISetupService.bootstrap()`, that runs in every grid's `onGridReady` and forces the **app-wide default theme (plain Quartz)** onto the grid via `setGridOption('theme', …)`.
- That ran **after** my `[theme]="themePink"` binding and silently overwrote it — so the RFQ grid kept rendering bare Quartz (blue accent, IBM Plex Sans). That's why the deploy showed "no change."
- **Fix:** re-assert `themePink` right after `bootstrap()` in the RFQ grid's `onGridReady`. I proved it on your live grid before committing — you saw it go pink (pink Add Product button, pink selected row, frozen Price Code seam, status pills, restyled headers).

## Next step for you
Once the deploy of `4fb92f65` finishes (a few minutes, same Cloud Build pipeline), **hard-refresh** (`Ctrl+Shift+R`) the RFQ page on `alsand2.designflow.app` and it will look like the screenshot above — permanently this time. The temporary version I applied for the test will disappear if you reload before the deploy lands; that's expected.

Want me to **watch the live site and tell you when `4fb92f65` is actually serving** (I'll poll for the new bundle hash), so you don't refresh too early?

Let me also save this gotcha so it doesn't bite future grid work:

input.content:
---
name: rfq-grid-theme-override
description: designflow-frontend grids ignore the [theme] input because a shared service overrides it on gridReady
metadata:
  type: project
---

In `popcre/designflow-frontend`, binding `[theme]` on `<ag-grid-angular>` does NOT
stick by itself. `GridUISetupService.bootstrap()` (src/@vex/services/grid-ui.service.ts),
called inside every grid's `onGridReady`, runs `gridApi.setGridOption('theme', <global default>)`
(default `quartz` from `GridThemeEventsService`) and re-applies it on every theme-change
event — overwriting the per-grid `[theme]` input.

**Why:** This is why the RFQ Theming-API reskin (themePink) rendered as bare Quartz
(blue accent / IBM Plex Sans) even though the theme was bound and deployed.

**How to apply:** To give one grid a custom theme, re-assert it AFTER
`gridUISetupService.bootstrap(...)` in that component's `onGridReady`:
`this.gridApi.setGridOption('theme', this.rfqTheme)`. The app also still imports the
legacy `ag-theme-alpine` CSS globally, but there is no `provideGlobalGridOptions({theme:'legacy'})`,
so grids default to the v35 Quartz Theming API. RFQ brand theme lives in
`grid-themes.config.ts` (`themePink`). See [[rfq-reskin-spec]].

content.text:
Clear answer now. I verified the actual JavaScript your browser loaded, and **the re-assert fix (`4fb92f6`) isn't in it yet** — the running RFQ chunk is still `chunk-WQN2ZKGU.js` from the *previous* build (`5c6f9e7`), and the re-assert code is absent from every loaded script. There's also no service worker, so this is **CDN/deploy propagation lag**, not a code problem:

- The version label flipped to `4fb92f6`, but the actual app bundle being served is still the prior one (which has no re-assert → bare Quartz → only the font-ish difference you noticed).
- I re-confirmed the fix is correct: re-asserting themePink just now turned your grid fully pink **and it held** (no late override). So once the real `4fb92f6` bundle is served, it will render the full reskin.

**What this means for you:** give the deploy a few more minutes to fully roll out, then do a hard refresh. If your pipeline/CDN is slow to swap the hashed chunks, that's the lag — nothing more to fix in the code.

I can confirm precisely when it's live: I'll **poll `alsand2.designflow.app`, locate the new RFQ chunk, and check that the re-assert code is actually being served** — then tell you "refresh now." Want me to watch and ping you? (I'd check every ~2 minutes.) Or if you'd rather, refresh in ~5–10 min yourself and tell me if it's still off, and I'll dig further.

content.text:
Good news first: your latest deploy worked — the theme **is** live now. On the left I can see the pink **Add Product** button, the **pink selected row** with the frozen Price-Code seam, the density toggle, and status pills. So the "make themePink actually render" problem is solved.

Now, the disconnect you're pointing at is real and it's important to name honestly:

## The right side isn't a reskin spec — it's a from-scratch redesign mockup

The two images differ **structurally**, not just in color. The right-hand "RFQ Prototype" is an idealized, hand-built mockup of an *entire redesigned app*. The left is your **real application** with its real structure, real data, and real backend — reskinned in place. Theming an AG Grid can change colors/fonts/spacing; it cannot reshape the page. Here's where they diverge:

| Area | Right (prototype) | Left (your real app) | Closable by theming? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **App navigation** | Left sidebar (Dashboard, Item Library, RFQ, Users…) | Top dark bar, hamburger menu | ❌ No — that's the whole-app shell, not the RFQ grid |
| **Detail "Factory quotes" panel** | Bespoke card: "4 quoted · lowest $1.79 · target landed $2.31", green lowest price, **MOQ/Location** columns | Plain AG Grid: NAME/PRICE/LEAD/CBM/PRICE TERMS/FOB COUNTRY | ⚠️ Partly — card styling yes; **columns are backend-driven** (MOQ/Location don't exist in your data) |
| **Columns** | PIC color swatches, CUSTOMER, LICENSE | Real columns + real data | ⚠️ Cosmetic only; data/columns are real |
| **Sample data** | Clean fake rows (Ceramic Mug, Evergreen Toys) | Your actual RFQ items | ❌ It's your real data |
| **Colors / pills / density / seam** | — | Now matching | ✅ Done |

**So the core answer:** the design team built a polished *vision* of the page (new layout, new nav, a custom-coded quotes panel, invented columns, sample data). The handoff I was given (SPEC_V2) framed the job as "reskin the existing AG Grid and its chrome" — and that's what's now applied. The colors match; the **architecture** doesn't, because matching the architecture means *rebuilding* the page and components, plus backend column changes — not theming a grid.

The genuinely closable gaps (within the real grid) that aren't fully there yet: header styling (uppercase/weight), zebra rows, the **detail card header with the "lowest $X" summary line**, green lowest-price highlight, and chosen-row pink border (SPEC §5k — coded for but not rendering). I can push those further.

To aim my next effort correctly:

options.description: Implement the §5k 'Factory quotes' card: summary header ('4 quoted · lowest $X'), green lowest-price, chosen-row pink border, card styling. Stays within the real (backend-driven) columns.

options.description: Push headers (uppercase/weight/bg), zebra rows, scenario group bands, mono numerics closer to the prototype.

options.label: Rebuild layout to match
options.description: Larger effort: left-sidebar nav + bespoke quotes component + column changes. Affects the whole app and needs backend work — well beyond an RFQ reskin.

options.label: Stop — reskin is enough
